perturbation.original_text: 4. (a) Carrier shall, at its sole cost and expense, procure and maintain liability insurance with a reputable and financially responsible insurance carrier or carriers properly insuring Carrier against liability and claims for injuries to persons (including injuries resulting in death) and for damage to property in amounts not less than the Minimum Levels of Financial Responsibility for Motor Carriers prescribed by the U. S. Department of Transportation (49 CFR (S)387 et seq.).
perturbation.changed_text: 4. (a) Carrier shall procure and maintain liability insurance. The specific terms of the insurance policy will be at the Carrier's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to maintain insurance at least at the level prescribed by the U.S. Department of Transportation violates federal regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 49 CFR ¬ß 387.303 mandates specific minimum levels of financial responsibility (insurance) for motor carriers. By removing the requirement to meet these minimums, the modified text contradicts this federal regulation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: eCFR :: 49 CFR 387.303 -- Security for the protection of the public: Minimum limits. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the carrier to maintain liability insurance at least at the levels prescribed by the U.S. Department of Transportation, as stated in 49 CFR ¬ß 387 et seq. The original text explicitly requires adherence to these minimum levels, while the changed text omits this requirement, stating that the specific terms of the insurance policy will be at the Carrier's discretion. This omission directly contradicts the federal regulation outlined in 49 CFR ¬ß 387.303, which mandates specific minimum levels of financial responsibility (insurance) for motor carriers, creating a legal contradiction as per the Omission Legal Contradiction definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, eCFR :: 49 CFR 387.303 -- Security for the protection of the public: Minimum limits, supports this by referencing the specific CFR section that mandates minimum limits.

perturbation.original_text: I. FILING OF CLAIMS (a) Claims in writing required. A claim for loss, damage, injury, or delay to cargo shall not be paid by Carrier unless filed with Carrier in writing, as provided in subparagraph (b) below. (b) Minimum filing requirements. A claim must be filed with Carrier within thirty (30) days from the date the shipment in question was delivered, and (i) contain facts sufficient to identify the shipment (or shipments) involved (ii) assert the grounds for Carrier's liability for alleged loss, damage, injury, or delay, and (iii) request payment of a specified or determinable amount of money.
perturbation.changed_text: I. FILING OF CLAIMS (a) Claims in writing required. A claim for loss, damage, injury, or delay to cargo shall not be paid by Carrier unless filed with Carrier in writing, as provided in subparagraph (b) below. (b) Minimum filing requirements. A claim must be filed with Carrier within fifteen (15) days from the date the shipment in question was delivered, and (i) contain facts sufficient to identify the shipment (or shipments) involved (ii) assert the grounds for Carrier's liability for alleged loss, damage, injury, or delay, and (iii) request payment of a specified or determinable amount of money.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the claim filing period to 15 days is unreasonable and likely violates the Carmack Amendment, which sets standards for cargo claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: 49 U.S.C. ¬ß 14706 (the Carmack Amendment) establishes the liability of carriers for loss, damage, or injury to property they transport. While it doesn't explicitly state a minimum claim filing period, courts have interpreted it to require 'reasonable' periods. 15 days is unreasonably short.
perturbation.location: ITEM 400: CLAIMS FOR CARGO LOSS OR DAMAGE, Section I

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 49 U.S. Code ¬ß 14706 - Liability of carriers under receipts and bills of lading | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! √ó No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 49 U.S. Code ¬ß 14706 - Liability of carriers under receipts and bills of lading U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) General Liability.‚ÄĒ (1) Motor carriers and freight forwarders.‚ÄĒ A carrier providing transportation or service subject to jurisdiction under subchapter I or III of chapter 135 shall issue a receipt or bill of lading for property it receives for transportation under this part. That carrier and any other carrier that delivers the property and is providing transportation or service subject to jurisdiction under subchapter I or III of chapter 135 or chapter 105 are liable to the person entitled to recover under the receipt or bill of lading. The liability imposed under this paragraph is for the actual loss or injury to the property caused by (A) the receiving carrier, (B) the delivering carrier, or (C) another carrier over whose line or route the property is transported in the United States or from a place in the United States to a place in an adjacent foreign country when transported under a through bill of lading and, except in the case of a freight forwarder, applies to property reconsigned or diverted under a tariff under section 13702. Failure to issue a receipt or bill of lading does not affect the liability of a carrier. A delivering carrier is deemed to be the carrier performing the line-haul transportation nearest the destination but does not include a carrier providing only a switching service at the destination. (2) Freight forwarder.‚ÄĒ A freight forwarder is both the receiving and delivering carrier. When a freight forwarder provides service and uses a motor carrier providing transportation subject to jurisdiction under subchapter I of chapter 135 to receive property from a consignor, the motor carrier may execute the bill of lading or shipping
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the claim filing period from thirty (30) days to fifteen (15) days. The Perturbation Explanation states this reduction is likely a violation of the Carmack Amendment because it could be deemed unreasonable. While neither scraped snippet specifies a minimum number of days, the Law Explanation states that courts have interpreted the Carmack Amendment to require 'reasonable' periods, and that 15 days is unreasonably short.

perturbation.original_text: VIII. DISPOSITION OF UNIDENTIFIED PAYMENTS, OVERCHARGES, DUPLICATE PAYMENTS AND OVERCOLLECTIONS NOT SUPPORTED BY CLAIMS If Carrier does not have sufficient information with which properly to apply an unidentified payment, Carrier shall notify the payor of the unidentified payment within 60 days of receipt of the payment and request information which will enable it to identify the payment. If Carrier does not receive the information request within 90 days from the date of the notice, Carrier may treat the unidentified payment as a payment in fact of freight charges owing it, subject to the regular claims procedure of this Item.
perturbation.changed_text: VIII. DISPOSITION OF UNIDENTIFIED PAYMENTS, OVERCHARGES, DUPLICATE PAYMENTS AND OVERCOLLECTIONS NOT SUPPORTED BY CLAIMS If Carrier does not have sufficient information with which properly to apply an unidentified payment, Carrier may treat the unidentified payment as a payment in fact of freight charges owing it.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to notify the payor of an unidentified payment, the Carrier can immediately treat the unidentified payment as the carrier sees fit which is illegal. There must be an effort made to reach out to the payor.
perturbation.law_explanation: 49 U.S. Code ¬ß 378.6 requires that a carrier notify a payor within 60 days when there is insufficient information to properly apply an unidentified payment. By removing the requirement to notify the payor, the carrier does not comply with the law.
perturbation.location: ITEM 410: CLAIMS FOR OVERCHARGE, DUPLICATE PAYMENT OR OVERCOLLECTION, Section VIII

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 49 CFR ¬ß 378.6 - Claim records. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! √ó No thank you 49 CFR ¬ß 378.6 - Claim records. CFR prev | next ¬ß 378.6 Claim records. At the time a claim is received the carrier shall create a separate file and assign it a successive claim file number and note that number on all documents filed in support of the claim and all records and correspondence with respect to the claim, including the written acknowledgment of receipt required under ¬ß 378.7 . If pertinent to the disposition of the claim, the carrier shall also note that number on the shipping order and delivery receipt, if any, covering the shipment involved. [ 47 FR 12804 , Mar. 25, 1982, as amended at 62 FR 15425 , Apr. 1, 1997; 83 FR 16225 , Apr. 16, 2018] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Carrier to notify the payor of an unidentified payment within 60 days. This directly contradicts the Law Explanation which states, "49 U.S. Code ¬ß 378.6 requires that a carrier notify a payor within 60 days when there is insufficient information to properly apply an unidentified payment." The Perturbation Explanation also supports the contradiction by stating, "By removing the requirement to notify the payor of an unidentified payment, the Carrier can immediately treat the unidentified payment as the carrier sees fit which is illegal. There must be an effort made to reach out to the payor." Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unintelligible and Scraped Law Snippet 2 makes no mention of the 60 day requirement, the other evidence overwhelmingly suggests that a clear contradiction exists.
